course_title: Life, Death, and Entertainment in the Ancient World

learning_outcomes.description: Students develop a comprehensive knowledge of the lives, beliefs, and customs of underrepresented marginalised ancient social and cultural groups as they study topics related to the themes of life, death, and entertainment in the ancient Mediterranean world under Roman rule from the last century BCE to the 3rd- 4th centuries CE.

Students acquire advanced discipline skills through working critically, comprehensively, and ethically with diverse types of ancient evidence, and with a range of ancient and modern historical, archaeological, and material culture methodologies.

Through the critical evaluation of diverse types of ancient evidence, theoretical models, methodologies, and ideas, students test and innovatively challenge traditional assumptions and biases about marginalised social and cultural groups below the level of the Greco-Roman elite perpetuated by ancient literary texts and modern misconceptions.

By engaging deeply and productively with both examples of ancient Greco-Roman and Australian indigenous written and visual storytelling, and with evidence produced by these indigenous communities, students become culturally aware citizens capable of positive social change who question traditional assumptions and beliefs about the creation and transmission of history.

Students demonstrate respect for cultural and social diversity by challenging traditional assumptions about both indigenous Australian and marginalised groups in ancient Greco-Roman society. By studying indigenous methodologies and visual material created by Australian indigenous communities in tandem with evidence about the lives of marginalised ancient peoples, students develop deeper respect for Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Indigenous peoples’ values, cultures, and knowledge.

Students demonstrate discipline-based and transferrable advanced-level research, thinking, data management and analysis, and problem-solving skills to produce high quality oral and written work for different audiences using evidence-based forms of persuasion.

Students are encouraged to become respectful, inspirational leaders by working collaboratively and independently on problems which raise ethical questions about how the history of the ancient and modern worlds has been shaped or recorded, particularly in relation to marginalised ancient social and cultural groups, and indigenous Australian and Torres Strait Islander groups.

Alongside developing advanced transferrable workplace skills in digital technologies, students reflect upon the ethical implications of applying different methods of communicating the outcomes of historical and archaeological research to different audiences.

assessments.due_date:
27/07/2026 - 26/10/2026

More information about Tutorial Activities is available on Blackboard.

Students must participate in a minimum of 5 out of the 10 tutorials or receive a mark of zero. Notes cannot be submitted in lieu of participation without a medical certificate or SAP.

Please note that it is a School of Historical and Philosophical Inquiry requrement that all assessment worth 15% or more must be attempted, so failure to attend any tutorials will result in an automatic grade of 2
assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Tutorial Workshop Activities

Further information on Tutorial Workshop Activities will be available on Blackboard under the Assessment Tab


Students are assessed on participation in tutorial workshops by completing the set readings, contributing to, and helping to foster discussion based on both these readings and the evidence discussed in class, and by participating in skill building tasks in person.

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course
assessments.submission_guidelines: In-Class Participation and in-class submission where required.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
3/08/2026 - 2/11/2026

Research Essays and Posters are based around Tutorial or Lecture Topics. Please see Blackboard for Topics and Due Dates
assessments.task_description:
Writing for different audiences is an important, transferable workplace skill that is important not only outside the University, but also in academia. For example, preparation for participation in meetings, workshops, seminars, and conferences usually requires preparatory reading and the preparation of concise reports, presentations, posters, and/or applications, many of which have strict word-limits. Some reports are also reflective in nature, asking about how an experience has helped you build skills or look towards future development. We will be practicing concise and reflective writing skills in tutorial workshops.

This two-part task requires you write a research essay (Part 1) and then turn it into a poster for a different audience Part 2).


Essay topics and further details are available on Blackboard and will also be discussed in class.


After receiving feedback on their posters, students are not required to rewrite them, but must correct any spelling, grammar, and factual errors if necessary after they are marked and resubmit for printing.

Posters will be printed and displayed at the Annual Ancient History Day organised by our UQ Alumni Group, The Friends of Antiquity.


Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers. An AI declaration coversheet must be used for both the essay and poster.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit through Turnitin (Essay) and Blackboard Assignment Links as instructed on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
